CSCE 641 Syllabus

Section 600 (58437)

Computer Graphics

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 11:10AM

End Time: 12:25PM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ZACH 590

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Nima Khademi Kalantari

Email:  nimak@tamu.edu

Office Location:  PETR 406

Phone:  (979) 862-4251

Office Hours

TR 4:00 - 5:00

Catalog Description

Computer Graphics. (3-0). Credit 3. Representation of 3-dimensional objects,

including polyhedral objects, curved surfaces, volumetric representations and CSG

models; techniques for hidden surface/edge removal and volume rendering;

illumination and shading; antialiasing; ray tracing; radiosity; animation; practical

models; techniques for hidden surface/edge removal and volume rendering;

illumination and shading; antialiasing; ray tracing; radiosity; animation; practical

experience with state-of-the-art graphics hardware and software. Prerequisite:

CSCE 441 or approval of instructor. Cross-listed with VIZA 672.

Additional Course Details

Course overview:  This course provides an in-depth introduction to physically

based rendering, with an emphasis on Monte Carlo techniques. We will study how

light interacts with objects in the real world, how image formation is mathematically

formulated, and how these formulations are solved in practice using modern

rendering algorithms. The course focuses on the theoretical foundations of

rendering as well as their application in practical image synthesis systems.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 441 or approval of instructor. Cross Listing:

rendering as well as their application in practical image synthesis systems.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 441 or approval of instructor. Cross Listing:

VIZA 672/CSCE 641.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Understand the physical and mathematical principles underlying light

transport, image formation, and physically based rendering, including

radiometric quantities and the rendering equation.

Implement core rendering algorithms such as ray tracing, direct illumination,

and global illumination methods.

Analyze the sources of bias and variance in Monte Carlo rendering algorithms

and evaluate the impact of different sampling and acceleration strategies on

rendering quality and performance.

Design and implement a physically based rendering system or rendering

component that integrates multiple techniques covered in the course.

Special Course Designation

rendering quality and performance.

Design and implement a physically based rendering system or rendering

component that integrates multiple techniques covered in the course.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Recommended

Physically Based Rendering: From Theory to Implementation (4th edition)

Authors:  Matt Pharr, Wenzel Jakob, and Greg Humphreys

URL for Resource:  https://pbr-book.org/4ed/contents

This material Is: Recommended

Robust Monte Carlo Methods For Light Transport Simulation

Authors:  Eric Veach

URL for Resource:  https://graphics.stanford.edu/papers/veach_thesis/thesis.pdf

Grading Policy

Programming assignments (70%):  There will be 4 programming assignments in

C++. One of the assignments is just setting up the environment and finishing a

simple task. So there is only 3 main assignments in practice.

Final project (30%):  Students need to implement additional rendering features not

simple task. So there is only 3 main assignments in practice.

Final project (30%):  Students need to implement additional rendering features not

covered in the assignments. They can work individually or in teams of up to 3

students. Students will propose their project at the middle of the semester. The

final project report will be due on April 30th.

Grading Scale:  A = 90-100, B < 90 and >= 80, C < 80 and >= 70, D < 70 and >= 60, F

< 60

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

You will lose 20% from each assignment for each day that it is late. However, there

will be 5 granted late days for the entire course. You are free to use it for any of the

assignments (note that, you CANNOT use it for the final project!). You will not get

will be 5 granted late days for the entire course. You are free to use it for any of the

assignments (note that, you CANNOT use it for the final project!). You will not get

any bonuses for any of the unused late days. All the assignments are due at 11:59

pm on  Canvas  unless otherwise stated. Note that, one minute over and 23 hours

over both count as one full day.

Additional Course Information

Academic Integrity:  The assignments in this class are individual unless otherwise

stated. For the individual assignments, all code must be written by the student. If

specified in the assignment instructions, the use of external libraries for performing

basic operations is allowed. However, using any external source code is NOT

permitted. Collaborating with other students beyond general discussion is also NOT

allowed. In particular, viewing other students’ code and/or written answers is strictly

prohibited. Posting your code on publicly accessible websites (e.g., GitHub), even

after the assignment deadline, is also NOT allowed.

AI Statement

The use of large language models such as ChatGPT, Copilot, or Claude is not strictly

prohibited. However, these tools may be used only for debugging or clarification

AI Statement

The use of large language models such as ChatGPT, Copilot, or Claude is not strictly

prohibited. However, these tools may be used only for debugging or clarification

purposes, not for generating code. You are expected to write all code yourself, and

copying or modifying AI-generated code is not permitted. If you use any AI tool in

any capacity while completing an assignment, even to ask a conceptual question,

you must submit a transcript of the full interaction along with your work.

The instructor may request a meeting with any student to discuss their code and

assess their understanding. If a student is unable to adequately explain their own

code, it will be considered an honor code violation and reported to the honor

council.
